Review: Fox Podium Fork - Heavy, Expensive, Upside-Down, & Excellent

Jun 24, 2025
by Dario DiGiulio  
Share
Tweet
You must login to Pinkbike.
Don't have an account? Sign up

Inverted forks have a long and meandering history in the mountain bike world, but they've rarely succeeded on a large scale in anything but a dual-crown application. There have been plenty of boutique offerings for folks looking to try the upside-down life, but Fox is now entering this space with their newest fork, the Podium.

The Podium isn't a replacement for the 38, so much as an attempt to make the lowest-friction fork they could. In fact, the initial concept wasn't even an inverted design, it's simply what they settled on to achieve the goal.
Podium Details
• 160 or 170mm travel (150mm OE only)
• 29" (44mm rake)
• Grip X2 damper with specific tune
• Glidecore air spring
• Steel 20x110mm Boost axle
• Weight: 2695g (170mm)
• Price: $2,000 USD / $2,679 CAD / €2,399 EUR / $3,349 AUS
• ridefox.com


Details

The Podium's most obvious detail is the inverted architecture. Large, 47mm upper tubes are machined from 7000-series aluminum, as is the generatively-designed crown. The tapered steer tube fits to a frame no differently to a standard fork. Axle to crown heights are equivalent to a 38 of the same travel, so bike fit and geometry shouldn't change if you were to swap from one to the other. The stanchions are 36mm in diameter, with most of the fork's fore-aft stiffness coming from those beefy uppers.


Aftermarket availability is limited to 160mm and 170mm travel variants, with a 150mm option available for manufacturer spec only. Similarly, you'll only be able to buy the Kashima-coated Factory edition aftermarket, as the black-stanchioned Performance Series Elite is also OE-only. Beyond the stanchion coating, the only other differentiator between Factory and PSE is the stanchion guard material: Factory spec gets carbon fiber, PSE gets thermoplastic.

The primary reason we're not seeing Podiums with more than 170mm of travel comes down to weight - adding any more travel necessitates higher chassis stiffness, and that meant adding even more weight to an already heavy fork.


By flipping the fork on its head, a few targets that typically require careful optimization become more easily achieved. Unsprung mass is reduced significantly, as the lowers of a traditional fork are replaced by the lighter stanchions of the Podium. Oil immersion is a constant thanks to gravity, and precludes any need for bypass channels in the design. Air release buttons are still useful, as the Podium can still build up plenty of casting pressure with altitude or weather changes, but those are now found at the top of the crown, tucked into the adjusters.

The brake mount is 200mm native, and can take up to a 230mm rotor. The brake housing routes through a series of 3 guides to make sure it stays quiet and out of the way as the fork cycles. There isn't a fender in production for the Podium yet, but Fox is promising one by the fall. The current design mounts into the lower hole of the steer tube, like an old THE fender.


Torsional stiffness is a common complaint with most inverted forks, and unsurprisingly became a key detail for the team at Fox to get right. In pursuit of matching the feel of the 38, they ended up with a 20mm steel axle that uses a DH hub standard, eliminating any unwanted flexibility. In fact, the option that ended up making it to production wasn't the stiffest design they tested, but was the one preferred by test riders.

To fit the beefier axle standard, you'll need a different front hub than you'd run in other single crown forks, or in some cases just a new set of end caps. Fox's sister company Race Face is offering end cap kits to adapt some of their existing Atlas and Vault hubs, detailed here.


The last internal detail to touch on comes down to the bushings, and more specifically the amount of overlap they've achieved inside the Podium. For the numbers people, that increase clocks in at 32% more bushing overlap than the 38 and 7% more than the 40, with 175mm spanning the upper and lower bushings. The point here is to better brace the fork under load, and keep things moving smoothly despite heavy compressions, rough terrain, and braking loads. The engineering team at Fox chose not to implement a sliding trombone bushing, as they found that reducing the distance between the axle and the first bushing had a greater impact on overall friction. Coupled with the very stiff uppers assembly, and you've got a very precise fork that feels smooth in extreme situations.


Damper & Air Spring

The Podium piggybacks on Fox's recent release of their Glidecore air spring, which uses elastomer rings to decouple the air spring from any chassis flex that might occur during riding. This is meant to isolate the spring's motion, and prevent any binding under hard riding. Pressure adjustment now takes place at the bottom of the fork, as does volume spacer adjustment. I prefer flipping the bike to adjust pressure, as the air valve can spit a bit of oil if you connect and disconnect a pump with the wheel on the ground. Even when flipped, I'd recommend being careful and having a rag on hand to clean up any oil that might bubble up - you're awful close to the brake rotor, after all.

Featured in that inverted air spring is a new volume spacer, with a unique design relative to any of Fox's other forks. The Podium's spacers are flat little 5cc donuts, with an o-ring in the center hole to keep them secured to a post on the air cap. The smaller increment allows for more fidelity in volume adjustment, and the cassette-style tool access means you won't be wanting for any proprietary tools.

I've been happy with 99psi in the air chamber, and 2-3 volume spacers on the stack.

The new spacer.
Kebab situation.

The Podium runs a Grip X2 damper, with a unique tune to match the lower friction of the fork's chassis. It features heavier compression tunes help control the easier rate at which the fork can go through travel, and a rebound tune to match the lower unsprung mass and reduction of sliding friction.


In comparison to a Fox 38 with the X2 damper, I'm running the Podium's clickers a bit more closed off. From closed, I'm sitting at 2-4 clicks of low-speed compression (LSC), and 2-3 clicks of high-speed compression (HSC), with the former of the two changing a bit more depending on terrain and trail style. The rebound is where things differ a bit more. Where I'm usually a good bit faster than suggested on the 38, I've been erring slower on the Podium. Currently, my rebound settings are 3-4 LSR and 1-3 HSR, again from closed. I'm curious how the current rebound tune will scale as rider weight and corresponding air pressure goes up, but only time will tell.

Speaking with some of the team at Fox, it sounds like folks have settled all over the map on rebound settings, but it's worth noting that a few people in my weight range were similarly closed on high-speed rebound. For me, the more closed end of that spectrum has been preferable, but that's only relevant to this fork, on this bike, in the places I've been riding it. According to Fox, a few aftermarket tunes will be available for the damper - any changes there can be made at a Fox service center. If you were to warrant such a thing, having to make a fairly major edit to a $2,000 fork would be quite annoying, but hopefully that's not necessary.

Price & Weight

These are likely to be two of the main sticking points with the Podium, so I figured it was worth doing a bit of comparison to some other similar forks on the market.

Fox Podium: 2695g | $2,000 USD. 150-170mm travel.
Fox 38: 2381g | $1,249 USD. 150-180mm travel.
RockShox Zeb: 2324g | $1,159 USD. 150-190mm travel.
Fox 40: 2745g | $1,849 USD. 190-203mm travel.
Push Nine One: 2790g | $2,250 USD. 140-170mm travel.
Intend Flash: 2350g | $2,263 USD. 160-190mm travel.

The latter two forks remain untested, but are worth noting as they're two of the more prominent inverted single crown options on the market. Obviously the combination of price and weight has little to do with how well a fork performs, but it's good to get a relative sense of how things stack up.


Durability

Two months of ride time isn't long enough to comment on the long-term durability of a suspension product, but I can touch on my experiences so far. The primary concern is sure to be the relatively exposed stanchions, as the inverted design puts them closer to harm's way. On this front, the stanchion guards have done their job admirably, with no damage to the stanchions to report. In fact, the guards themselves are pretty unmarred, despite having a satin coated carbon finish that readily shows marks. Obviously freak accidents happen, but so far it seems the rocks and debris have been kind.

The brief I received on the service procedure sounds fairly standard, and perhaps even easier than a standard fork. The lack of a combined lowers assembly means you can service one leg at a time, minimizing oil loss and focusing on one task at a time. I have yet to perform this myself, so I'll report back once the job is done. We'll see how this shakes out in time, but I have a theory that the fork will ingest less contaminants, as the seals are pointed down and tend to collect less grit and grime.

Otherwise, the Podium is pretty standard, with no other maintenance tasks to keep in mind. One little detail to keep an eye on is the front brake hose routing, as the line needs to be set with enough slack for the fork to cycle without any binding or bending - just pay attention when first installing and check every now and then.


Ride Impressions

I've been riding the Podium consistently for a couple months now, and also had the opportunity to spend time on a copy of the fork earlier this past winter. In both cases, the fork has been nothing short of impressive, with a level of grip and sensitivity that can almost be hard to get used to. That grip comes without much of a penalty, especially once I honed in my damper settings. I found that the Podium benefits from heavier damping adjustment, and can ride on that damper to provide support. There were instances early on where I thought I'd stiffen the spring to achieve the desired feel, but was able to hit those targets through damper adjustment.

The most unique sensation provided by the Podium comes into play in rough terrain under heavy braking - something plentiful in the Sea to Sky. The big fork feels like it's moving through travel where others would start to halt a bit, keeping the wheel on the ground and comfort in the hands. In loose, rough, trashy bits of track, I've found you can keep a bit less weight on the hands and let the fork work to keep things in check. By no means is this a profound difference from a Fox 38, but subtle gains add up in situations like these.

Stiffness
I'll touch briefly on stiffness, as it has been anything but an issue over the testing period. Torsionally, the fork feels very similar to a 36 - while I'm sure there are some subtle differences that could be picked up in machine testing, I'm left feeling like they both keep my wheel going where I want it to, without any surprises. Fore-aft, the Podium feels more like a DH fork, holding shape through successive hits and big one-offs impressively well.


Comparing Fox's Options
Riding the Podium in the bike park feels like an ideal use case, as does pedal-access terrain where you'd potentially opt for a downhill bike. The 170mm single crown is excellent, but still won't convince you that it's a 200mm Fox 40, which still takes the cake for just trucking through the roughest terrain. Having ridden the 38, Podium, and 40 all within the past week, I think they all have their places. The 40's is obvious, but the line between the 38 and the Podium is a little more blurry. If pure bump-absorbing performance and grip are your ultimate metrics, then the Podium is the winner. If a balance of weight, price, and performance are more equally weighted, then I'd opt for the 38. The 38 isn't some wimpy lightweight option, and still feels like an excellent option on all manner of gnarly trails.

Climbing & More
Besides the additional weight, there's not much that the Podium does to impact climbing performance. Sure, improved bump absorption plays a role on the way up as well, but not nearly as much as it does on the way down. I wouldn't go putting the Podium on an otherwise light trail bike, as the added weight might impact the character of a snappy bike. That said, I do think there will be some interesting shorter travel builds that implement the 150mm Podium.

I imagine the Podium will be a common sight on all sorts of eMTB builds, particularly for those looking to perfect their moto cosplay, but I'm more curious to see which pedal-powered mountain bikes choose to feature the extra 300-400g of fork. For me, it feels like a perfect fork for the Frameworks Enduro I've been riding, matching the heft and descending capability of the bike very well.

Jessie-May's Take
For those curious as to how the Podium performs under a smaller and lighter rider, we have Jessie-May Morgan's thoughts on a 160mm Podium she's been riding. Her first impressions are as follows:

I rode the Podium fork at 160mm on a Yeti MTe on a mix of bike park and enduro trails around Leogang and Saalbach Hinterglemm. The day amounted to 4,350 meters of descending, or 14,000+ feet. I mention it because that's the biggest day of descending I've bagged since I tore a rotator cuff tendon. It was also the most comfortable I've been on a bike since that accident. Its suppleness did not leave me wanting.

I'll not be drawing final conclusions from a single ride, and will continue to test the fork at home, with a full review to come on how this fork performs for lighter riders (I weigh 57 kgs or 126 lbs). I ran 66 psi in the air spring with two volume spacers. I went to full travel twice, feeling a distinctly metallic clunk at the bottom. That said, there is room for a heap more spacers in there to dial in the progression.

As for the damping, I ran more compression damping than I typically would on the Fox 38, settling on a low speed setting close to the middle of the range, with the rebound close to fully open on the high and low speed adjusters.

Throughout the stroke, the fork felt incredibly smooth and traction was ample. In high speed berms I felt nothing untoward with the steering, and any concerns over a potential lack of torsional stiffness were, for me on that day at least, unfounded.—Jessie-May Morgan


Pros

+ Stellar downhill performance
+ Best in class grip
+ No stiffness compromise


Cons

- Very expensive, very heavy
- 20x110mm wheel requirement
- Damping range might be too limited for some




Pinkbike's Take


The Fox Podium isn't for everybody. The weight, cost, and limited travel offerings make it something of a specialty item, but boy is it good. For the intended purpose, it may just be the best single crown fork I've ridden, taking the category one step further.— Dario DiGiulio


Visit the high-res gallery for more images.

StanWu01 (Jun 25, 2025 at 8:45)
 I am pretty mechanically inapt, especially with suspension. I also don't have a clean work space and good tools for rebuilding suspension parts. So I always just buy good condition used forks and shocks and send them to shops for rebuilds right away.
This is a good workaround for many OE QC issues that have been persistently present with many brands.

It's really sad that many pedal bike parts are not even as "plug and play" as their motor sports counterparts

brycebee (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:25)

Fix-the-Spade (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:40)

  • 320
shirk-007 FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:41)
 @mikekazimer: Want to really feel old? Some of us rode rode QR20. A quick release 20mm axle in old Z1 and Z3's. I only feel old if I go and count how many years ago that was.
  • 910
andelinc FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:46)
 Really? I've never met anyone who has ever broken a 15mm axle. I've always thought it was backwards that the majority of rider weight most of the time is on the rear axle and yet a 12mm seems to suffice, but then for some reason we need much bigger axles on the front.
  • 181
vinay FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:03)
 @andelinc: For single (or no) front arch forks, the stiffness of the axle is more called for than for the rear where the tubes of the rear triangle are more spread out to help with bending and torsional stiffness.

20x110 is getting rare these days indeed but 20x110 boost is still out there, isn't it?
  • 20
tremeer023 (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:26)
 @shirk-007: yeh, I had a first edition dj1 that was QR20. 2004 I think.
  • 20
R-M-R (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:40)
 @andelinc: i.kym-cdn.com/photos/images/original/000/538/731/0fc.gif
  • 30
andelinc FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 11:13)
 @vinay: Didn't think about the lack of front arch. That makes perfect sense.
  • 40
groghunter (Jun 24, 2025 at 12:00)
 not mention an air cap straight out of Marzocchi's forks 15 years ago. it's almost like the force of the tool should go through the mass of the part, instead of across a void with tiny little wrench flats spanning it.
  • 60
skylerd (Jun 24, 2025 at 12:56)
 @andelinc: 20mm is not for strength, but rather adds a lot of stiffness for a very small weight penalty.

masters5 (Jun 24, 2025 at 13:37)

Dougal-SC (Jun 24, 2025 at 14:18)
 Ironically it was Fox and Shimano that created the completely un-necessary 15mm axle over 20 years ago. Because the Fox 32 would have needed a new crown and lower legs to fit a proper 20mm and keep the offset it had as QR.

@mikekazimer You're really getting old when you think it's only 15 but it's more than 20 years!

likeittacky (Jun 24, 2025 at 19:21)

NorCalNomad (Jun 25, 2025 at 9:57)

speedfreek (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:29)
 "the first inverted single crown fork from a major manufacturer"

Oh, kids these days... Shiver SC, Dorado SC, RS1, Lefty Ocho. All inverted single crowns, all from sizeable manufacturers. This is just the first time Fox have done it. I'm sure it'll be better than all of those others, but to say it's the first from a major manufacturer is not correct.
  • 142
vinay FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:08)
 Magura Boltron has also been out there though short lived. I'd say Intend is probably more mainstream in this market than this fork. Rconroy (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:25)

Notmeatall FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:27)

cooperquinn-wy (Jun 24, 2025 at 11:23)

vinay FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 11:30)

gnarnaimo (Jun 24, 2025 at 11:36)

vinay FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 11:56)

  • 30
bok-CZ FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 11:58)
 Major manufacture, so maybe RS.
  • 31
speedfreek (Jun 24, 2025 at 11:59)
 @vinay: I'd forgotten about that Atomlab fork. Those things were mental! IIRC they weighed about as much as the rest of the bike they were bolted to, haha! I had also completely forgotten that Magura one - Super cool, but never really made much of a splash.

Anyway, for those pointing out the ones I missed: My point was more about calling out the assertion by @Dario-DiGiulio that this is the first inverted single crown fork from a MAJOR manufacturer. Which it isn't.

I flippin love weird forks, and those from Atomlab, Intend, Push, Wren etc. are all really cool. But I'm not sure I'd count them as MAJOR manufacturers given how non-mainstream they are (I could count on one hand how many Intend or Push products I've seen in real life on the trails, whereas just about everyone else runs RS or Fox)

Also, Cannondale moto was dual crown, was it not?
  • 94
chrismac70 FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 12:17)
 PB don’t count anything not made by fox
  • 60
bigtim FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 12:37)
 @speedfreek: yes, Cannondale moto was dual crown. Source: I'm looking at the set on my classic Super V DH right now!
  • 40
Steffn FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 12:41)
 Probably arguably what a major manufacturer and not just a sizeable manufacturer is.
  • 20
speedfreek (Jun 24, 2025 at 13:05)
 @Steffn: Sizeable in terms of production and how mainstream their products are. Sorta means the same as "major" really, doesn't it?

Oh, and while I'm at it, X-Fusion did an inverted single crown back in 2014 too. They're not known for their high-end stuff, but given how prolific their products are I'd count them as a major manufacturer.
  • 50
speedfreek (Jun 24, 2025 at 13:10)
 @bok-CZ: RS for sure, but at the time of the Dorado SC and Shiver SC I'd argue that Manitou and Marzocchi were both considered major manufacturers, and still are to some degree...

CullenHerring (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:53)

Lagr1980 FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:55)
 @llaama: we all know how this works.. main brands , reviews. etc.. The Intend is a work of art, precision.. Push one seems the same level... Fox, dont think so.. but as they want a piece of the pie.. they play catch up now that it is more accepted.
  • 11
mrti (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:56)
 I will stay with the Original for sure.
  • 161
Comatosegi (Jun 24, 2025 at 12:15)
 @llaama: Totally different budgets and volume. Fox has a full marketing team and can dish out review units to all the big reviewers ahead of time. Push and Intend are high-end, low volume and don’t have marketing teams with large budgets to throw out a ton of review units out to journalists and even then do they have excess supply to make that worth it. I agree that the Push/Intend value proposition is much better now.

chrismac70 FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 12:17)
 Because it says fox on the stanchion so will win whatever the other forks are like

CobyCobie (Jun 24, 2025 at 12:18)

GTscoob (Jun 24, 2025 at 13:22)
 @llaama: does PUSH need a review to sell their forks? They seem to be running at optimal capacity. Any more hype would just increase lead times and make their premium customers wait longer.

sfarnum (Jun 24, 2025 at 14:14)
 @Lagr1980: They just gave the formula fork a great review. The idea that big advertisers get good reviews and small brands don’t is dumb.

llaama (Jun 24, 2025 at 21:52)

JohSch (Jun 25, 2025 at 0:08)

GTscoob (Jun 25, 2025 at 9:09)
 @llaama: Pinkbike is pretty proactive about getting products in-hand that they want to review or being responsive to manufacturers selling buzzworthy products. Sometimes manufacturers just don't need to send review products. How long did EVIL go without sending a review bike, yet that didn't hurt their sales.

If you like the way PUSH suspension rides, you aren't even considering this Fox fork. Their stuff rides well and there's a reason they have so many repeat customers.

Ryan2949 FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:07)
 Nothing about this product makes sense lol.
  • 40
kiisseli (Jun 24, 2025 at 22:33)
 Around 2800 USD in Europe :-)
  • 5410
KUNTHER (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:12)
 At this price it would foolish not to just go for a Push fork. At least you won't need a new front wheel to fit the Push fork.
  • 236
cccab (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:30)
 Definitely. The Push or the Intends for sure. The Intends might be a closer comparison since they're air, and it's incredible how Fox can claim they are the stiffest single crown. There is not a single thing Fox has over the Intend (weight beats the Push but air vs coil isn't a fair comparison) and if this becomes more popular than those forks, marketing has well and truly won. Or Kashima hype I guess.
  • 49
airsoftesneeto (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:34) (Below Threshold)
 that 15mm axle compared to a 20mm steel axle is going to be very noticeable in a positive way. If someone is going to pay 2250 for a Push fork then whats another 250 to get a wheel and a podium fork?
  • 50
jpnp22 (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:36)
 I was thinking the same thing. The weight difference is negligible and you get coil performance and consistency at that.

Sethimus (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:51)

  • 100
KUNTHER (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:59)

stranjbikes (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:01)

Sethimus (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:04)

tallaznballer (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:04)

  • 34
airsoftesneeto (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:21)
 @KUNTHER: i wasnt saying 250 for another wheel. the podium is 2000, the push is 2250. pay a total of 2500 on a fork/wheel which is 250 more than a push fork. see what i am saying? You can get a strong aluminum front wheel for 500
  • 10
airsoftesneeto (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:22)
 @Sethimus: interesting, i would want to see a side by side review
  • 161
cccab (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:45)
 @airsoftesneeto: If you follow what Cornelius of Intend and Darren of Push have been sharing, how the hub interfaces with the dropouts is more important that axle size. Both companies made a conscious decision to end up with 15mm over 20mm. Of course you can choose be skeptical of their claims but these forks, especially the Intends, have been out for awhile and no one has complained of any stiffness issues. You'd have quite a shitstorm on mtbr by now if that's the case.

I don't doubt that the Fox podium may need the 20mm axles though, their crown construction probably isn't as beefy as the Edge/Flash or 9.1.
  • 90
hamncheez (Jun 24, 2025 at 11:20)
 At this price and weight just get a fox 40
  • 62
airsoftesneeto (Jun 24, 2025 at 16:03)
 @chaoscacca: good point about the crown. Intend's crown i think is forged which is definitely going to help with how strong it is but also comes at a weight penalty i would think.

I feel like though you guys are knocking it before you try it though. I mean look at what Dario said, its possible the best single crown fork he has ever ridden.

I think this fork looks rad and i dont think FOX would just do a 20mm axle to literally make everyone just buy a front wheel for the sake of it. Think about it, not selling it as a 15mm boost axle is a HUGE barrier to entry and yet they chose a 20mm boost axle. That says a lot.
  • 142
cccab (Jun 24, 2025 at 18:33)
 @airsoftesneeto: Intend Edge/Flash are significantly lighter than the Podium. Depending on tax/exchange rate, cheaper too. Cheaper, lighter, stronger, Fox has done the impossible (for Intend).


Dario has a fine line to balance here, PB can't piss off their biggest brands. Officially PB hasn't reviewed the 9.1 or Intend forks, so he can say that. I don't doubt the podium is a good fork. It is possibly better than other RSU single crowns, but something like a well tuned mezzer can't be too far off, at maybe half the price. At this price point they compete with Push and Intend, and very likely bring an inferior product to the table, despite being quite late to the party.

Sethimus (Jun 25, 2025 at 4:42)

sethtc (Jun 25, 2025 at 13:02)

  • 5817
mrbrighteyes FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:09)
 So...

20x110 required
Poor fender placement at best, annoying access to air chamber adjustment.
50g lighter than a dual crown (with less stiffness and smaller stanchions)
$2k

I guess it's cool that Fox is pushing the envelope, but this doesn't seem like it's going to replace anyone's 38 or ZEB.
  • 211
Notmeatall FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:26)
 manitou seems to be the optimal inverted fork.
  • 151
bat-fastard (Jun 24, 2025 at 11:14)
 @Notmeatall: I've 2 sets of Dorado's, running them on ebike at 180mm gives axle to crown same as a170mm single crown too. They just unreal.
  • 37
wburnes (Jun 24, 2025 at 13:37) (Below Threshold)
 @Notmeatall: @bat-fastard: only things I don't like about my Dorado:

1. 203mm brake mount instead of 200mm.

2. The dials on mine are all way too stiff and the clicks are difficult to notice. Might be better if it just used an Allen key to adjust instead of knobs/dials

3. I have to have the bike off the ground to get an accurate air pressure. So I'm balancing the bikes bashguard on my knee in the parking lot while trying to pump it up. Brutal.

4. The dual chamber system is a bit too complicated for idiots like me, I wish it was electronic and set itself up.

I wish it was a little more than 203mm. 32" wheel compatibility would be cool too at some point

Wonderful fork though.
  • 41
Dougal-SC (Jun 24, 2025 at 14:23)
 @wburnes: I've got a 3D printed spanner for the Dorado compression knobs. One free with every custom tune. ;-) But once you've got them set you never need to monkey with them.
The Dorado Expert is single chamber air and 32" wheels are only new crowns away.
  • 20
bat-fastard (Jun 24, 2025 at 22:31)
 @wburnes: we have 3 sets, all clicks are good on knobs. I have a block of wood put between tire and crown for doing pressure, makes it very easy. Also have a block at 180mm that i use for ebike so no internal adjustments. The newer dual bolt crown is a big upgrade if your on the older singlebolt.
  • 20
ozhuck2flat (Jun 24, 2025 at 22:55)
 @wburnes: Can the 203mm brake mount be faced down to to 200mm? It's only 3mm. How deep is the screw tapping?
Frames with poor production finishing from factory can have their rear brake mounts fixed. I wonder if these can too...?
  • 31
Dougal-SC (Jun 25, 2025 at 1:52)
 @ozhuck2flat: just run a 203mm rotor. There are plenty to choose from.
  • 40
bat-fastard (Jun 25, 2025 at 2:45)
 @Dougal-SC: just run a hope vented 220mm.
  • 31
Dougal-SC (Jun 25, 2025 at 2:55)
 @bat-fastard: or any other 220/223.
  • 21
KJP1230 (Jun 25, 2025 at 8:24)
 I will say, I watched a handful of reviews from pretty major reviewers yesterday, and they all gushed about this fork for nearly identical reasons.

Huge increase in support from the dampers, which had less tradeoffs in performance than seeking support from the air spring. Significantly better front end grip and small bump compliance. Noticeably less hand fatigue. Better support in berms and hard cornering. Less binding and noticeably smoother and more predictable suspension movement.

Suspension products are already great. If this product could actually deliver a 10-20% performance improvement over the Zeb or 38, it would justify the cost and new hub. Its also important to note that while the USD chassis is a major piece of this, it also includes a redesign to a "floating" airpsring as well as a new implementation of the damper tuning. Its essentially a "ground up" new product which may be markedly different than existing USD forks on the market.
  • 31
mrbrighteyes FL (Jun 25, 2025 at 8:36)
 @KJP1230: "Huge increase in support from the dampers, which had less tradeoffs in performance than seeking support from the air spring. Significantly better front end grip and small bump compliance. Noticeably less hand fatigue. Better support in berms and hard cornering. Less binding and noticeably smoother and more predictable suspension movement."

These are all just marketing talking points, especially if they're the same over multiple reviewers.
  • 21
KJP1230 (Jun 25, 2025 at 8:50)
 @mrbrighteyes: Maybe. Maybe they're true.

This fork does have markedly more bushing overlap than other forks, a newly designed/tuned damper specific for the USD design, as well as a "floating" internal air spring. The purpose of these designs are specifically targeted at the exact performance attributes that people are noticing.

In general, I have had very positive experiences with overwhelmingly well reviewed products in mountain biking. If I see one or two "pay to play" reviews, I chalk it up to marketing budget. When I see universal praise from 6+ reviewers of varying size, etc. I tend to become more interested in the potential product upsides. One of the reviewers did an on trail test with slow-mo cameras (for example) to show both the lateral stiffness compared to other forks, as well as specific on-trail examples of improved performance when compared to the 38.
  • 20
TEAM-ROBOT (Jun 25, 2025 at 15:30)
 @KJP1230: Who did the slow mo test? Curious to watch. -Charlie who did the Podium Review for Vital
  • 40
KJP1230 (Jun 25, 2025 at 15:48)
 @TEAM-ROBOT: Jeff Kendall-Weed has the review of the Fox Podium up. The flex test happens at about minute 4:20 (nice!).

KJP1230 (Jun 25, 2025 at 15:53)

TEAM-ROBOT (Jun 25, 2025 at 16:19)

bigtim FL (Jun 25, 2025 at 1:05)

BurtMcBurburt (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:05)

succulentsausage FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:30)

panthermodern FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 23:21)
 Correct me if I'm wrong, as I was out of mountain bikes for a number of years, but didn't fox essentially kill the 20mm axle only now to bring it back years later?
  • 21
BurtMcBurburt (Jun 24, 2025 at 23:57)
 Idk, last single crown Fox I can think of was the 36 vanilla back in 2014ish... After that fox claimed that the 36's with 15mm boost axles were just as stiff in testing as the 20mm ones... and I believe them, but the bigger axles just feel better, look better, doesn't add much weight, and so many hubs nowadays can be easily converted to 20mm with end caps, I just don't see the point of not taking advantage of the larger axle standard.

Muscovir (Jun 25, 2025 at 6:30)

  • 230
frorider2 (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:22)
 “ Torsionally, the fork feels very similar to a 38 - while I'm sure there are some subtle differences that could be picked up in machine testing”. Spitballing here—how about pinkbike finally measuring key attributes at a lab?
  • 230
mashrv1 FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:31)
 There’ll be a German doing that good work anytime now…
  • 611
frorider2 (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:48) (Below Threshold)
 @mashrv1: yes of course they will, I just like to make fun of PB’s laziness. I understand why the low budget / tiny staff review sites don’t obtain independent lab measurements, but PB has a large audience. Anyway Enduro mag ride test indicated they felt the slight lack of torsional rigidity in steep sharp turn situations, further reinforcing the idea it’s comparable to a fox 36 (not the 38 as Seb qualitatively concluded) on this particular attribute.
  • 101
mammal (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:05)
 Sounds like you're asking for a subscription based content model, to pay for lab testing...
  • 132
the-forfather FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:35)
 @frorider2: What are you going to get from lab tests when they have tested it out in the wild? Sure, lab tests are great for the spec sheet, but real-world testing is what we are all here for. I would rather have a user experience than a graph showing percentages or whatever.
  • 81
chrismac70 FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 12:21)
 No chance. They can’t declare it the best if the numbers prove otherwise. Just like PB were the only review who claimed xtr was noisier than transmission
  • 20
groghunter (Jun 24, 2025 at 12:27)
 @leon-forfar: agreed, and torsional stiffness is something where there's a big window of acceptable. TBH i rode a shiver for over a decade, and while sure, i could hold the wheel between my knees and twist the bars, it never felt like an issue when actually riding the bike (a sentiment i am far from alone in expressing.)
  • 70
IsaacWislon82 FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 13:14)
 @leon-forfar: You get data to help nail down on-trail feel. For example "torsional stiffness wasn't as issue" could turn into "torsional stiffness was only X% lower than our 38 we tested, confirming our ride impressions". It can be a really amazing tool for understanding why things perform the way they do
  • 30
BenPea FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 13:55)
 @frorider2: Get in the queue. I'm still waiting for them to hire a sub-editor.
  • 11
the-forfather FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 14:19)
 @IsaacWislon82: Fair point, but again, that is mostly just spec sheet info and is only really useful for the marketing department and when developing. The real-world testing here concludes that it's marginally better than a 38 with class-leading grip and a unique chassis over the already excellent forks it competes with that cost half as much. In other words, unless you are a racer chasing tenths or just want the newest and coolest, this isn't necessary. That's the info I'm looking for, at least.
  • 35
phops (Jun 24, 2025 at 21:52)
 Don't even need to test it. At 2695g, there is no way that there is enough material in there to make it torsionally stiff, and it doesn't have keyed stanchions, nor are the dropouts super wide with lots of clamp area. If a dual crown boat anchor of a Manitou Dorardo at 3600 grams has noticeable wheel flex, this thing will for sure have it.
  • 11
mammal (Jun 25, 2025 at 9:53)
 @phops: But if the fork functions at a high level, and feedback for this fork is that it does, is that even an issue?
  • 20
phops (Jun 25, 2025 at 10:32)
 @mammal: Depends on what kind of riding you do. Torsional flex is actually good for tracking through chunk. The fore aft stiffness of USD forks is also a big plus for freeride, where big hits onto the front end feel more controllable.

In the end, you should get about the same stiffness as the trail forks, but you have more weight up front, which is very noticeable if you are more dynamic rider.

A lot of these designs are being driven by the ever increasing number of ebikes, for which weight doesn't really matter. But then again, there is no reason to not have dual crowns on long travel ebikes.
  • 10
Dougal-SC (Jun 25, 2025 at 13:10)
 @the-forfather: Lab tests give objective data, not subjective opinions.
  • 10
mammal (Jun 26, 2025 at 7:10)
 @phops: Right, so not so much of an issue. Got it.
  • 10
phops (Jun 26, 2025 at 8:59)
 @mammal: USD forks never were bad, they are just unnecessarily heavy (or super flexy like XC forks when you try to make them lightweight). Fox themselves said this thing has comparable torsional flex to a Fox 36, same fork that used to be ridden in EWC at quite high speeds.

In a perfect world, all long travel enduro bikes would be dual crown compatible, so instead of running this you would just stick a 180mm dual crown fork on.
  • 10
mammal (Jun 26, 2025 at 10:40)
 @phops: Not disagreeing with any of that.
  • 10
the-forfather FL (Jun 26, 2025 at 10:57)
 @Dougal-SC: For sure, and I agree it's relevant. That info is usually a part of the press release and on the spec sheet, which we can all find and read. My point is that most people are here to read about how it performs in the real world because it's more relatable than a bunch of numbers, and the experience is harder to come by than the objective data. Lab development, and real-world testing are both necessary for creating a product. The lab only goes so far though. It's needed to start and build test-specific parameters of the product. Real-world testing pushes the product beyond lab conditions. Very few of us have a lab, but we all have the real world.
  • 23
Dougal-SC (Jun 26, 2025 at 15:14)
 @the-forfather: You don't get real-world reviews at product release time. Even new owners will give you rose-tinted glasses views of a product.

In my experience it takes 6 months for the hype to fade, the truth to become evident and the grumbling to start. I've lost track of the number of "best ever" product that owners started to totally hate it once the honeymoon period was over.

That process can be short-cut by publishing objective data. Objective data is also the kryptonite of marketing BS.
  • 21
the-forfather FL (Jun 26, 2025 at 16:43)
 @Dougal-SC: Sure you do. These forks have been in the wild for months despite only just launching. This review talks about how it rides out on the trails in depth. I think it's fair to say new owners might have rose-tinted glasses, but PB (and other publications) editors don't buy the forks they are reviewing. They are testing them and comparing them to the competition, and it's their job to do so without any/minimal bias. If anything, testers from media outlets would be best equipped to talk about how it actually rides relative to the competition, as they actually get to try different forks for extended periods of time. Regular consumers don't have the ability to buy and back-to-back ride most of the forks on the market. That's the main reason we are here, reading their reviews.

Again, I'm not saying the data isn't important, but it's only a portion of the story. A good example of why you can't ONLY follow the data is Red Bull Racing's F1 car. In their models/sims, it's capable of hitting X lap time for X amouunt of laps, yet on track, it's basically a backmarker/ midfielder unless your initials are MV. Another example would be EV mileage in the lab vs the real world. Correlation between the data and the real world is very important, but ultimately, you will face more scenarios in the real world. From what I've seen of the bike industry, those data points are used to fluff out the marketing BS rather than tear it down. Ultimately, both tests have their place, but I do think the vast majority of us here are more interested in user experience than it being X% this and that versus X fork.
  • 11
Dougal-SC (Jun 26, 2025 at 21:47)
 @the-forfather: They haven't been in the wild. They've had early versions available to specific advertisers and sponsored riders.
Those advertisers never, ever, say anything critical about a product like this. Because if they do they're never invited back.

Real user experiences take time. At least 6 months.
  • 11
the-forfather FL (Jun 26, 2025 at 22:18)
 @Dougal-SC: They have been in the wild under testers with both pre production forks and the production forks for at least 2-3 months: “ I've been riding the Podium consistently for a couple months now, and also had the opportunity to spend time on a copy of the fork earlier this past winter.” Pinkbike aren’t alone here either.

2 months with lots of laps is more than enough time to get acquainted with the performance of the fork and see if there are any signs of impending doom. Especially if it’s your job. Long term reviews are better done as updates to the original reviews. There isn’t much to know between 2 months and a year other than “there was x problem that arose, and this was how it was handled” or “still rides like the day we got it, or at least we cant notice a difference. Probably time for a service”. Sponsored riders should be taken with a grain of salt, but Pinkbike have put out negative reviews for bad products and aren’t paid for their reviews.
  • 11
Dougal-SC (Jun 27, 2025 at 5:08)
 @the-forfather: pre release product with tame advertisers is not the wild at all.

I know how this industry works and it is nothing like you portray.

It takes 6 months for hype to die and truth to come out for every new product.
  • 11
the-forfather FL (Jun 27, 2025 at 8:31)
 @Dougal-SC: I define “in the wild” as out on trails. A place where you can run into people riding said products. Maybe it’s worthy of an asterix as it is not uncommon to cross paths here with Jackson or Finn, and our local hills are frequently used by media outlets and industry people riding new products (can’t tell you how often I see stuff before the “spotted” articles hit PB). Pinkbike sometimes uses our shops workshop to build bikes.

Hype dies right away if the product sucks/ falls flat. 6+ month tests are import for long term durability for sure. For this specific fork, it uses already proven seals, an airspring and damper that have been out for a year+. Hopefully the chassis survives…

Ultimately, we can agree to disagree.
  • 11
Dougal-SC (Jun 27, 2025 at 13:46)
 @the-forfather: in the wild means with the public.

Hype takes 6 months to die even when the product sucks immediately. Many examples of this.
  • 12
the-forfather FL (Jun 27, 2025 at 13:56)
 @Dougal-SC: Guess where mountain bike trails are? Guess where the roads to the trailheads are?

Sure, they can be as sneaky as they can be, but they don't own the land they are testing on. Hop over to NSMB, and you can see in their Podium article that they first saw a Podium on a bike in the back of a pick up truck in February (roughly when I found out about it) in a parking lot lol. Sure, it's "pre-production", but that is just final tweaks being made to the damper tune, with the hardware/ graphics being production spec. I'm not even going to debate this anymore, as my sources are my friends who work at Fox.

I don't even know what point you are trying to make about "Hype". Hype is marketing. Sure, it's a shiny new unconventional fork, and will have more interest now than it will once it's been around for some time. That goes for every single thing ever, regardless of whether it's good or not. You're point must be in regards to long term durability, and how that could affect the initial reviews. That is fair, but like I said, unless something goes seriously sideways, there's not much information to be gained there. But we can agree to disagree on that.
  • 11
Dougal-SC (Jun 27, 2025 at 20:20)
 @the-forfather: you're never going to understand this are you?
  • 240
rcrocha (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:37)
 This will definitively improve my riding a ton. It is not a want it is a need. Wait until my 15 followers see my sick clips tearing up the gnarly greens and blues on one of these. Taking it to the next level!
  • 241
tristanpalmer (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:13)
 Shiver me timbers
  • 150
frorider2 (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:31)
 After reading the review in enduro mag, learned some things: fork crown is available in 68 and 58 mm tall versions (I assume PB reviewed 58 mm version??). Torsional stiffness claims from Fox are ‘comparable to Fox 36’, not the 38.
  • 120
OTBSteve (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:36)
 58mm and 68mm are in reference to crown diameter to better blend with large head tube eMTB. Does not change "height" or axle to crown. It's more for aesthetics than anything else.
  • 32
frorider2 (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:50)
 @OTBSteve: thanks! So when Enduro wrote ‘crown thickness’ they meant to write ‘steerer tube lower diameter’.
  • 60
OTBSteve (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:58)
 @frorider2: Does not change the steerer tube it's still 1.5 taper, but the part of the crown where its next to the head tube of the frame is a larger diameter. Still the same headset, it just blends better. You could run a 58mm crown on an eMTB with a large head tube, it will just look better with a 68mm as it blends better. Like wise you could run a 68mm on a standard enduro bike, but it will look really strange as the crown flange will be wider than the bottom of the head tube. I would say if you have a full torque eMTB with a large downtube battery you want the 68mm, otherwise you want a 58mm.
  • 10
the-forfather FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:34)
 Edit
  • 160
mtbthe603 FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:31)
 The "I love mountain biking so much" at 4:50 of the video from Dario was so genuine and how I feel every time I ride a new trail for the first time
  • 140
Inclag (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:03)
 @Dario-DiGiulio : What's the difference in mass between the lowers of a FOX 38, lubricating oil, and the spring and damper piston rods compared to this new fork? Magnesium is a pretty low density material. I've got a feeling I know which one is lighter and has less unsprung mass....
  • 30
thekaiser (Jun 24, 2025 at 12:52)
 Yeah, and that is not even counting the steel 20mm axle in the inverted fork vs a 15mm aluminum axle in the right side up ones.
  • 40
iamamodel (Jun 24, 2025 at 14:29)
 Thank you! Came here to say this. I wrote a long comment last time we were looking at this fork. This "Unsprung mass is reduced significantly" is said every time and everyone simply believes it to be true.

And, anyway, since it's essentially the same hardware, only upside down, what's the maximum difference? And is that difference going to be felt when you have a 2 kg wheel/tyre rotating at high speeds?
  • 150
spacedoutboy (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:39)
 My main take away is that @Dario-DiGiulio drives a Honda Jazz! Such an awesome car! It’s so versatile for such a compact car.
  • 210
Dario-DiGiulio FL Editor (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:41)
 The world would be a better place if everyone drove a Jazz.
  • 31
vinay FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:11)
 @Dario-DiGiulio: They say my motorcycle has half the engine of a Jazz. Does that count as half the effort?
  • 714
thenotoriousmic (Jun 24, 2025 at 12:41) (Below Threshold)
 @Dario-DiGiulio: Jazz is the absolute plague of the UK roads. They’re meant for young drivers but the only people driving them are people who got their driving licenses out of a serial box in 1961 and should have handed in their licences 15 years ago. You can’t get anywhere without nearly being killed by a jazz or something taking at least 3 times as long because you’re stuck behind a Jazz. Personally think trying to buy a Jazz should automatically disqualify you from driving.
  • 40
lwk (Jun 24, 2025 at 14:32)
 so I learned something on the PB comments today. I couldnt figure out what you were talking about but a Honda Fit in North America is called Jazz in other markets. Helped my daughter buy one as her first car. Bright yellow. and yes, it is an awesome little car!

suspended-flesh FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 20:49)

Dario-DiGiulio FL Editor (Jun 24, 2025 at 22:17)

vinay FL (Jun 25, 2025 at 3:14)
 This isn't really a small car, is it? Seems mid-sized to me, like a Volkswagen Polo. Our previous car was a 2007 Peugeot 107. Not too different from the Citroen C1 and the Toyota Aygo from that era (as these brands developed these cars together). Not sure about North America but they have been super popular in Europe. We have ridden it across battered Eastern European backcountry roads and it just wouldn't break. And whenever you do break something, it is relatively cheap to fix and spares are everywhere. But yeah the way to carry a bike was on the roof. Saw no issue really. If you can't lift a bike over your head, what's the point of winning a race?

Simann (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:46)

For fox to not offer custom tunes kinda sucks. Ive got an Intend fork on order, hoping it comes in as dialed as the Push!
  • 120
sprung-mass (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:35)
 The Intend Edge is a massive 570g lighter too!
  • 31
Simann (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:43)
 @sprung-mass: right!!! Thats enormous lol

But I must say part of the Ride characteristic of the Push is that the fork does add a little bit of weight to the front end which keeps it planted on the steeper climbs. As well, I think it helps the front end push into the ground for a little bit more traction! These forks are truly Enduro/Downduro only. If you on a trail bike go with a 36 or a lyric.
  • 10
sprung-mass (Jun 24, 2025 at 11:07)
 @Simann: For sure, regular telescoping forks will be staying for the lighter segments. The planted front end should really be the result of front center and rear center ratio.
  • 50
nicoenduro (Jun 24, 2025 at 12:22)
 @sprung-mass: and cheaper too! I think the next fork might be the Intend, for sure higher quality than the fox, cheaper, lighter, whaaat
  • 170
2supple (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:22)

  • 151
roo5ter (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:57)
 Why not add the Manitou Mezzer Pro to the comparison: 140-180mm travel & 2030g.
  • 110
heinous (Jun 24, 2025 at 15:59)
 I think the most impressive thing about this fork is that Push have made a similar fork entirely in the USA that's more easily serviced, travel adjusted, features a bottom out circuit, better mudguard placement, and it's only a fraction heavier and a fraction more expensive. It seems like if you were in the market for an USD trail/enduro fork the biggest thing holding back from buying the Push is a couple of reviews to validate it.
  • 20
chocolate-thunder (Jun 27, 2025 at 20:35)
 This is why Fox sponsors everything and everyone. They give the impression that they're better because that person whose riding you like is on a Fox (nevermind with a custom damper), and you, on a subconscious level, relate their skill to their equipment, and think "perhaps if I have the things they have..." Kids, it's not about the equipment (so much). If this succeeds, it'll only be because there are enough suckers out there to buy it. It won't succeed on merit, and it is overpriced for what it is. I'll be shocked if anyone actually chooses to pay MSRP for this when there are so many good alternatives that are higher quality, higher performance, and made likely more ethically. I wonder if seeing these in the wild will be a way to ID someone with the ideal combination of spare money and a lack of discernment/knowledge. I'm really trying to be nice here...
  • 131
howejohn (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:20)
 At that weight and price, if you want a low-friction fork just put a smashpot in a 38?
  • 30
thekaiser (Jun 24, 2025 at 13:10)
 It would be interesting to see someone quantify the relative contribution of the friction of the spring, damper, and stanchion/slider interface. Coil conversions are often stated to have a frictionless buttery feel, but if the air spring is only say, 20% of the total fork friction, then you still have 80% of the original remaining. Not saying a 20% drop is insignificant, but it still leaves you pretty far from frictionless.
  • 50
Rabbuit (Jun 24, 2025 at 23:32)
 @thekaiser: Try a smashpot, it does feel frictionless compared to a air spring. So much so you'll change your damping settings.
  • 2210
loud-socks (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:09)
 does the CSU on this creak after a month too?
  • 119
onemanarmy FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:17)
 Always trips me out when I hear this. I haven't had a creaking issue for years now and before that I didn't have any more creaking issues with Fox than any other brand of fork. Most of them creaked for the same exact reasons regardless of manufacturer. It's like people just jump in and post about problems that they've heard people have had before.
  • 41
loud-socks (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:26)
 @onemanarmy: so if every brand has the issue, that means it’s ok? I have personally experienced this with a fox fork.
  • 64
the-forfather FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:42)
 @loud-socks: It's not ok. That's why it was fixed and sorted out for MY2022+ Fox forks. I went through about 5 CSUs in 2.5 years from Fox before getting on the 38s. My current Fox 38 on my mai bike has been creak-free since I got it in 2021. The other 38s I've had on other bikes have also been creak-free. I hear mostly similar stories working at a shop.
  • 20
loud-socks (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:48)
 @leon-forfar: I’m glad it was fixed for you. For every brand of fork I’ve owned, CSU creaks. It’s not an issue of whether or not it will be warrantied, but time off the bike for a problem that can and should be fixed on the drawing board.
  • 40
gnarnaimo (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:50)
 @onemanarmy: Every single Fox fork I have had has had this issue. Including my 2024 Fox 38, 2024 Fox 36 and 2023 Fox 34.
I have only had to warranty one Rockshox fork because of this back in 2018 I believe.
  • 10
the-forfather FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 11:12)
 @loud-socks: I meant they addressed the issue. Not just mine personally. Our shop had an in-house suspension center that saw 6-8 forks serviced a day for 10 years, and pretty much all forks creaked prior to 2022, and it didn't matter the brand. One of my buddies who works at Fox mentioned they had a quiet update to the CSUs after the first batch of 38s, and it worked. I'm not saying it doesn't happen ever (there's always a percentage of failures), but the vast majority are now sorted. The guys getting theirs to creak tend to be the guys riding hard 4+ days a week, which, given the shore's reputation for eating bikes, is pretty solid.
  • 10
onemanarmy FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 11:58)
 @leon-forfar: 4 times a week in high moisture areas... Most folks really shouldn't have creaking issues anymore. And that goes for essentially every manufacturer because they essentially all had the same problem and the same fix. LOL!
  • 51
Dougal-SC (Jun 24, 2025 at 14:26)

onemanarmy FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 14:51)
 @Dougal-SC: I do like the little gold logo on the guards. Clean and simple.

sollie (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:43)

bproelofs (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:18)

Thegrumpymechanic (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:45)
 For the love of god, 20x110 is NOT A NEGATIVE!, but a MASSIVE positive. its stiffer and lighter.
  • 30
thekaiser (Jun 24, 2025 at 13:23)
 I mostly agree with you, although in this particular example, I doubt it is lighter, as the axle is steel. For 20mm aluminum, though, I fully agree.
  • 11
borisimobike (Jun 24, 2025 at 16:03)
 @Thegrumpymechanic its a "negative" in the way superboost 12x157 is a "negative"... to enjoy this fork you need to rebuild you wheel now around a new hub, unless you're with one that can take an adaptor... but I think its even more of an inconvenience than superboost as usually I'm buying bikes complete with wheels, where its not like this fork includes a hub/wheel. As for me, I'm prepared to pick up gently used 38's in the classified sections of my local marketplace on the cheap
  • 10
Thegrumpymechanic (Jun 27, 2025 at 11:30)
 @thekaiser: agreed caveat, for the same material.
  • 20
Thegrumpymechanic (Jun 27, 2025 at 11:36)
 @borisimobike: All hubs worth having are convertible between 110x20 boost and 110x15 boost. so only ~£10 'inconvenience' which barely counts.

15mm axles simply do not make sense, they are not lighter, they are not cheaper to manufacture, they should not exist in the first place.

157 is not a good comparison, as that entails compromise elsewhere of multiple aspects, not just the effect on hub spacing. Also 157 vs 148 isa different hub shell, where as already stated a well designed boost front hub can do either without difficulty.
  • 90
wolftwenty1 FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:09)

  • 50
bigtuna00 (Jun 24, 2025 at 19:51)
 Weird that you chose the Intend Flash to compare to. I suppose the logic was "just pick the heaviest Intend fork" but given that it offers 20mm more travel it's not really in the same class. The Edge would be more comparable, and makes it even more obvious how absurdly heavy this fork is (the Edge is 2160g with a 220 brake mount). Even the Hero (which goes to 170mm at ~1910g) would compare favorably, it's easily as stiff as any 38mm fork.

Really bummed this is how the Podium turned out I was hoping for a domestic inverted fork somewhat comparable to Intend. Ordering service kits from Germany is a little painful...glad I didn't wait and just ordered an Edge though.

chrismac70 FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 12:25)
 So what’s the difference in unspring weight v a 38. You say it’s lighter so how much lighter or is this straight from the marketing copy
  • 50
cambro (Jun 24, 2025 at 13:28)
 Curious as well, aluminium stanchions on this vs magnesium lowers and a steel 20mm axle and corresponding (possibly) heavier hub to suit vs aluminium 15mm axle

Surely can’t be that much difference in unsprung weight
  • 51
justinfoil (Jun 24, 2025 at 13:48)
 Why all the faff about the axle? It's the same 20x110 "Boost" as has been used on 40s and Boxxers for quite a while now. Yes, it's not going to fit _all_ the hubs currently used on trail and enduro bikes, but it will fit _many_ with new end caps.

It's also one of those things just is just not worth complaining about. It's progress, it's better, it's what it took to get the performance. Even for single crowns there is almost no compromise.

I haven't measured modern Boost 15 vs Boost 20, but I did back when the 36 still had a 26" version and double pinch-bolt dropouts, and the 20mm axle set up was lighter than the 15mm set up, considering the axles, dropout inserts for 15mm, and corresponding end caps for a Hope Pro 4. Perhaps a 15mm axle setup could be made marginally lighter with optimized dedicated dropouts, but a few grams savings on a 2 kilo fork isn't worth _any_ performance decrease.

Maybe with 110x20 being on yet another good fork, it'll trickle down to be an option on 38s, Zebs, 36s, and Lyriks.
  • 80
Binderclip FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 16:19)

  • 41
KJP1230 (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:43)
 You all seem to be missing the biggest selling point of this fork. According to the promo video released by Fox, I've been made to understand that with this fork I'll be able to ride like Brendan Fairclough. I'll take 2. One for my every day ride, and one for my Rampage bike. I expect to be receiving the call from Red Bull any day now. Frankly, I am stoked that this thing exists. Anything which noticeably improves performance - in this case front wheel grip, which is fairly important for mountain cycling - should be celebrated at this point in our sport. I'd expect to see more competitors to this product, which is good news for all of us.
  • 40
stranjbikes (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:46)

KJP1230 (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:53)

jesse-effing-edwards (Jun 24, 2025 at 11:11)

tbc (Jun 24, 2025 at 11:46)

dividebyzero (Jun 24, 2025 at 17:21)

wutamclan (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:38)
 Pinkbike comparison test idea: New Fox Podium vs used Fox 36/38 upgraded with Vorsprung Smashpot coil.

$2k vs $400
  • 40
drcrudlord (Jun 24, 2025 at 15:29)
 Or Mezzer, weight would be comparable in theory.
  • 10
poozank (Jun 25, 2025 at 11:22)
 great idea, I bet its splitting hairs at that point
  • 10
justinfoil (Jun 26, 2025 at 8:37)
 Smashpot alone is $400, upgrading a used fork is going to approach mrsp. Less than Podium, yes, but not $400
  • 20
wutamclan (Jun 26, 2025 at 10:50)
 @justinfoil: no I mean if your bike already comes with a 36 or 38 and you’re considering an upgrade to a Podium. For less than $400, a Smashpot coil upgrade will give similar performance? Maybe I’m wrong, hence idea for comparison test. Also a V1 Smashpot with coil, is less than $300 right now from Vorsprung.
  • 10
justinfoil (Jun 27, 2025 at 8:37)
 @wutamclan: It might give similar performance plushness-wise to some extent, but according to the goals of, and now reports of, the Podium, it's pretty buttery end under big loads, which the other forks just can't do because they have the smaller tubes where the biggest loads are.

Also, if you are really cross-shopping a $2K new fork with a $300-400 upgrade kit, perhaps the Podium just isn't a realistic goal and you should just get the Smashpot.
  • 10
poozank (Jun 30, 2025 at 20:56)
 @justinfoil: well, that's the whole point of the experiment - can you get close to a 2k fork for $400 and nothing to with a true cross shop
  • 20

In reality, who needs all the angle that SC forks permit?

nerdymtbiker FL (Jun 25, 2025 at 7:39)

  • 20
Bearorso (Jun 25, 2025 at 4:17)
 I'm disappointed in the travel offered - I had hoped that with this fork, Fox would offer a 190mm travel version to match the Zeb.

I'm an old Downhiller, and Life Long Dirt bike rider - I want the travel , or near enough ( and I ran a 9" Mr Dirts way back in 96, and, modified WB's to 225mm) of a DH bike as, despite my age, I ride very aggressively.

This is said to be the same axle to crown length as the 170mm 38, so, going by that, and the A/C of the 38, you'd have a 203.?mm 190. Within a poofteenth of the minimum AC advised for 40s.

Though, the extra travel would almost certainly bring to the weight, or just past it, of 40s. As it is, I run 40s on my E Frames, but, I'd like to have less of a chance of hitting my knees on the upper crowns - I'm missing my LH kneecap, and have knee joint replacements.

Something I also have wanted, is getting rid of the LH side Lower Adjuster protuberance, for something far lower profile / recessed. I've destroyed not just the removable cap, but also the actual adjusters, a few times over the last, near on / over 20 years of Fox running Fox 40s. Well, I've had some 44s (look them up, Kids), since the early 80s
  • 20
beeboo (Jun 25, 2025 at 19:21)
 Looks awesome but a 2k price tag is crazy! I wonder how popular this will be and if it will push other manufacturers to produce an USD fork too. I wonder if this will lead to an USD 40, similar to the one gee rode like 10 years ago
  • 40
jcav5 (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:12)
 is inverted really that much better? enough better to deal with all the other compromises?
  • 21
SJP (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:33)
 Why is it so heavy? The uppers are comparable to the lowers on a 38. The stanchions are skinnier than on a 38. The internals are basically the same. Is it mostly the 20 mm steel axle and associated hardware at the bottom of the stanchions, including the guards?
  • 41
thenotoriousmic (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:01)
 Don’t ignore the fact that though we’ve got used to it the zeb and the 38 are also way too heavy for how much flex they still have at the crown / steerer joint. A light weight double crown would still be a better option.
  • 60
ITBVolks (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:29)
 If I had to guess - no cast magnesium as with the lowers on all their other products. "Uppers" are likely thicker 6000 or 7000s series aluminum.
  • 40
CobyCobie (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:57)
 Uppers are probably wayyyyy heavier. Thicker and aluminum instead of magnesium.

SJP (Jun 24, 2025 at 13:44)

Dougal-SC (Jun 24, 2025 at 14:30)

stranjbikes (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:54)

farmer-john-2000 (Jun 24, 2025 at 16:43)
 It’sa sexy fork for sure, but I think Push make tye USD fork to beat…

ZeGermans FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 16:49)
 @Dario-DiGiulio thanks for the review. Is fox going to release the 38 with the Glidecore air spring? I’ve been holding out for the USD fork, but it’s a little pricey for me, so I’m hoping a revision of the 38 will be released with the latest tech.
  • 21
Mtbdialed (Jun 24, 2025 at 16:53)
 I can attest to the USD thing being legit af! I just build a Supernaught with an EXT Vaia USD fork. it's so good, it's hard to put to words....the best analogy I can give is, it makes a Fox40 feel like it belongs on a walmart bike. I am not embellishing.
  • 20
seanestes (Jun 24, 2025 at 17:34)
 just to be clear, Podium is 314g heavier than 38, not 500g as noted in the video. The figures in the text of the article are accurate, however the callout in the video is not. It is heavy, but it is NOT that heavy.
  • 21
dododuzzi (Jun 25, 2025 at 9:20)
 This seems like a bad copy-cat of the PUSH nine-one, but at a ridiculous weight. It weights the same of the PUSH, with the difference that the PUSH is a COIL fork! Pinkbike does not even see i. At close to a pound more than a Fox 38 (!) this is not a fork for a bicycle, this is designed for the new breed of 120nM motored-cycles that are polluting trails.
  • 10
gabriel-mission9 (Jun 25, 2025 at 9:52)
 Id love to know what the actual % difference is in unsprung weight @Dario-DiGiulio. A set of 38 lowers with a relatively burly brake/wheel/tyre combo fitted (and I guess whatever oil lives down in the lowers for the sake of accuracy) vs the same setup fitted to a pair of podium lowers.

I find it hard to believe that the difference will be much at all with all the other unsprung weight factored in.

To be completely honest, I wouldn't be that surprised if the aluminium lower legs, steel 20mm axle, (plus the possibly larger bearings of a compatible hub) air spring topcap and vol spacers etc actually works out a touch heavier than the magnesium lower casting of a traditional fork plus an air spring shaft, ali 15mm axle and smaller bearings. But yeah, as I say, I doubt the % difference is much to write home about either way.
  • 10
dick-pound (Jun 26, 2025 at 8:08)
 @Dario-DiGiulio the price comparison is incorrect because it is a mixed of VAT prices vs non-VAT. For example, the Intend Flash price is including the DE VAT which is 19%. Their actual price for non EU countries is actually around USD 1915 at the current exchange rate so way cheaper than the Fox Podium.
  • 10
nichecyclingDOTch (Jun 26, 2025 at 22:48)
 We have an intend Edge testfork... Test both and decide.
  • 10
wildebeest5 (Jun 27, 2025 at 6:01)
 It's a great looking fork, until you put a mudguard/fender on it - then (IMO) it starts to look daft. I also presume that with said mudguard being so far from the wheel (except at max travel) (who's at max travel with the bars turned?!) that it will mean more mud in the eyes for us folk from the moist countries. therefore, I'm out.....(even if I did have 2k burning a hole in my pocket)
  • 21
90sMTBEnjoyer (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:02)
 So, they brought out the 36sl and 36. I'm guessing the right side up 38 is the "sl" and this is the burlier offering. Also, has anyone measured the stanchion diameter? If so, what is it?
  • 80
borisimobike (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:11)
 It says in the article the stanchions are 36mm, with the stiffness coming from the massive uppers.
  • 40
EnsBen (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:14)
 "The stanchions are 36mm in diameter, with most of the fork's fore-aft stiffness coming from those beefy uppers."
  • 10
henrycant FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:20)
 says in the article that its a 36mm stanchion
  • 20
90sMTBEnjoyer (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:24)
 @borisimobike: Ah, I missed that, thank you. Fox's website doesn't say it so I was confused. Interesting to see that despite the fork only having 36mm stanchions, many reviews are saying it's as stiff as a dual crown! If they made a dual crown version of this with slightly thicker stanchions I can imagine it would be a very stiff and precise fork.
  • 20
thekaiser (Jun 24, 2025 at 13:17)
 @90sMTBEnjoyer: With upside-down forks, there is typically a massive increase in for/aft stiffness, and a substantial reduction in torsional stiffness. I could see this fork being comparable for/aft to dual crowns like a Boxxer, but torsionally, it is more like a single crown Fox 36, at best.

Interestingly, Fox did have a dual crown upside-down prototype fork back in maybe 2012 or so, that some of their pros like the Athertons and Gwin were testing, but they said they could never get it torsionally stiff enough for those riders preferences, without making it unviably heavy with a solid steel axle and huge dropouts.
  • 53
26forlyfe (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:03)
 Pinkbike any substance behind this rumour WBD will discontinue juniors? www.youtube.com/watch?v=BbAp8pr5cls&t=1532s&ab_channel=MovingTheNeedlePodcast
  • 40
shadow20 (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:20)
 @brianpark please sell us the levy milk carton shirt!
  • 20
hellbelly (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:51)
 Buy whatever existing fork and a $1000 of pastries? I am in!

Also "the first production inverted mountain bike fork"? Marzocchi Shiver SC would like a word.
  • 66
Dougal-SC (Jun 24, 2025 at 14:42)
 This fork now means every major manufacturer has produced a single crown USD fork. Manitou Dorado SC, Rockshox RS-1, Marzocchi Shiver SC and RAC, Magura/WP Boltron. Every one of those single crown USD forks was discontinued because the same brands (except Magura) had conventional forks that were lighter and stiffer!

Great to see a mainstream 20mm axle option though. Every fork should be using that instead of 15mm. The crown being scalloped out towards the crown though makes no sense. That's the highest bending moment on the whole fork and should be full width across the steerer tube.

The GRIPX2 damper range is interesting. Stock the HSC and HSR on GRIPX2 do very little and LSC not much either. So testers LSC/HSC/HSR position can be pretty much ignored with air pressure and LSR position being all that matters.

Stock GRIPX2 is massively overdamped for a 57kg rider, so a firmer tune will be even worse.

I see the biggest direct competitor to this fork not being from Intend, but rather the dual-crown Manitou Dorado Pro. For less money you get a carbon DC USD fork which can be spaced from 200mm travel to whatever you want and is only a few hundred grams more.
  • 36
DServy (Jun 24, 2025 at 15:22)
 "The GRIPX2 damper range is interesting. Stock the HSC and HSR on GRIPX2 do very little and LSC not much either. So testers LSC/HSC/HSR position can be pretty much ignored with air pressure and LSR position being all that matters.

Stock GRIPX2 is massively overdamped for a 57kg rider, so a firmer tune will be even worse."

What are you even talking about? You say that there's nothing HSC and LSC do and then say that its over-damped. Pick one!
  • 90
pmhobson (Jun 24, 2025 at 15:39)
 @DServy: a fork can be overdamped and at the same time its adjusters only provide a narrow range of configuration.
  • 31
Dougal-SC (Jun 24, 2025 at 15:57)
 @DServy: lots of damping but the adjusters don't change it much.
  • 11
DServy (Jun 25, 2025 at 8:16)
 @Dougal-SC: I've seen the dyno charts and the knobs do what they need to. There's only so much range an adjuster can/should have before you need to change the underlying tune.
  • 20
Dougal-SC (Jun 25, 2025 at 11:39)
 @DServy: I have my own dyno and the knobs do not do what they need to do.

The majority of damping is coming from the midvalve which is not externally adjustable.

smootherthenyou (Jun 24, 2025 at 9:37)
 I always curious, does 2k fox that much better $300 OEM performance series or MZ?

I have Fox and MZ suspension on my bike tuned for frame / my weight (one on other in service and vise versa) and cannot feel the difference in terms of trail performance, just more or less knobs to play with and knobs size.
  • 71
onemanarmy FL (Jun 24, 2025 at 10:29)
 If you're looking at a bike with a $300 p series or other low end fork then this fork is not for you.

The answer is pretty simple... Is an M3 worth the extra money over a 330? Doesn't matter because the person shopping for the M3 isn't even looking at the 330 and the person looking at the 330 doesn't need the M3.

Is Kashima worth paying a few hundred dollars for? If you're talking about performance to dollars... probably not... but if you're buying a Kashima fork you don't care.

Is a Rolex really 10x the watch that an Omega is? Probably not. But if you're stressing about $5k for an Omega you're not the target market for Rolex any how.

This fork is for people that have money to burn. The people that want the newest best thing regardless of price. Or people that want the best performance in the segment and don't care about price.

If you're window shopping $2200 bikes then a $2k fork isn't for you.

This fork is for someone not stressing about spent $14-20k on an e-bike.

voodoo666 (Jul 2, 2025 at 9:44)
 All the talk about the damper, volume spacers, settings, and yadda, yadda.. yet no mention of the "crown diameter" which is really the part the matches the lower part of the headtube on a frame. Hey make sure it looks good, yet geeze more SKUs. And also... "Generative design", the words written yet nothing else, which is AI designed using an AI assisted FEA (look that one up) which is finding it's way into lots of CAD design lately. This is cool and why no explanation I'm wondering. Many other reviews also just threw out the term with nothing else. This beast of a fork is already heavier and I feel sorry for analog powered bikes, yet keep shuttling and sitting on a lift.. ho-hum. For an EMTB this could be the $$$$ of even more cost, yet a possible less maintenance choice in a fork. I would spend more to have it not fail like everything else on my EMTBs.

 @Mtbdialed: tell me you hate traction without telling me you hate traction
  • 22
Mtbdialed (Jun 25, 2025 at 10:29)
 @lukemech: I have ridden coil forks...they pale in comparison to my new EXT Vaia. also, a coil DH fork weighs more than the EXT. so less performance and more weight. not exactly the selling points you might think they are.
  • 32
lukemech (Jun 26, 2025 at 5:28)
 @Mtbdialed: your personal experience means much to you, but statistically, it's essentially irrelevant. First of all, weight. As we see with weights being attached to DH bikes, it's more about the balance and WHERE the weight is, as opposed to the actual number. I'm talking g specifically about the ratio of sprung to unsprung mass, and the balance overall. Second, using an air spring always involves more seals. Seals create friction, which increases hysteresis, mimics damping, but in a far more variable form both of which are completely undesirable in a suspension product. The bottom line is no matter how well an air system works, converting to a coil will always improve it. Air is better for most people, because most people do not want to spend the time and money dialing in their spring rate when the alternative is just 3 minutes with a shock pump, and consumer still believe that lighter is better. Coil will always be the choice for maximum performance.
